title: The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
subtitle: The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.
caption: Between 1980 and 2010, the raw female-to-male wage ratio in the United States rose across the tenth, fiftieth, and ninetieth income percentiles. The line for the fiftieth percentile begins near 60% in 1980 and climbs steadily to just over 80% by 2010. The tenth percentile line starts slightly higher, around 65%, and follows a similar upward trajectory, ending near 82%. The ninetieth percentile line begins at roughly 62%, rises to approximately 75% by 1990, and then flattens, remaining near that level through 2010. All three lines show an overall increase over the thirty-year period, with the most rapid growth occurring between 1980 and 1990. The gap between the tenth and ninetieth percentiles narrows over time, while the fiftieth percentile line crosses above the ninetieth percentile line around 1998.
